extractedEntities.name: Type-Safe Service Communication

keyInsights: Witchcraft is Palantir's Java microservice framework that standardizes service communication via Conjure-generated interfaces.
Health and logging APIs are defined declaratively using Conjure IDL and shared across all Witchcraft-based services.
Witchcraft provides URI live-reloading and connection pool reuse, enabling dynamic service discovery without restarts.
The witchcraft-api repository demonstrates Palantir's infrastructure-as-code approach to API contract management.

title: GitHub - palantir/dialogue: A client-side RPC library for conjure-java

extractedEntities.name: Dialogue
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's open-source client-side HTTP RPC library designed to work with Conjure-defined APIs, providing concurrency limiting, client-side load balancing, retries, and streaming.

extractedEntities.name: Conjure
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's interface definition language and code generation framework for type-safe API definitions.

extractedEntities.name: Witchcraft
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Java microservice framework that provides client creation abstractions on top of Dialogue.

extractedEntities.name: Concurrency Limiter
extractedEntities.description: AIMD (additive increase multiplicative decrease) concurrency limiter in Dialogue that prevents bursty traffic from overloading upstream servers.

extractedEntities.name: Client-Side Node Selection
extractedEntities.description: Dialogue's load balancing approach where routing decisions happen in the client, eliminating the need for an L7 proxy and its latency penalty.

extractedEntities.name: Content Decoding
extractedEntities.description: Dialogue's support for JSON, SMILE, and CBOR content formats by default, with extensible user-defined encodings.

extractedEntities.name: Client-Side Load Balancing
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where service clients make load balancing decisions locally rather than relying on a centralized L7 proxy.

extractedEntities.name: Zipkin Tracing
extractedEntities.description: Dialogue's built-in distributed tracing using Zipkin-style spans and X-B3-TraceId header propagation.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Conjure-generated service interfaces (blocking and async variants) that Dialogue uses as the basis for type-safe RPC communication.

keyInsights: Dialogue is Palantir's HTTP client library that implements client-side load balancing, eliminating the need for L7 proxies.
Dialogue uses AIMD concurrency limiters and FIFO queuing to handle traffic spikes without overloading upstream servers.
Conjure generates both blocking (FooServiceBlocking) and async (FooServiceAsync) Java interfaces that Dialogue consumes.
Dialogue supports live URI reloading without losing concurrency limiter or node selection state.
Content encoding supports JSON, SMILE, and CBOR natively, with streaming for requests and responses.

title: Slate - Overview - Palantir

keyInsights: Slate is a drag-and-drop application builder that can create public-facing applications without server or DNS configuration.
Slate seamlessly integrates with the Ontology, using object data, Functions, and Actions alongside external data sources.
Applications built in Slate support full CSS customization for corporate branding and custom design experiences.
Slate enables non-Foundry users to submit data into Foundry via public Internet applications with validation logic.

Deployments leverage multi-availability-domain and multi-fault-domain architectures for high availability.
Foundry's key components include data integration, ontology, decisions, modeling, analytics, application building, and core services.

Palantir's commercial model uses fixed-fee, outcome-aligned contracts rather than traditional per-seat licensing.

title: Palantir Foundry Compute Module For Absolute Beginners

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's containerized workload system that allows developers to run custom Docker containers for data generation, processing, and streaming within the platform.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform where compute modules run and produce output to streams and datasets.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Ontology SDK used at DevCon for building against the dynamic ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Pipelines Module
extractedEntities.description: A compute module type that is auto-granted permissions to its input and output resources, simplifying data pipeline setup.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Output
extractedEntities.description: A streaming data output resource that compute modules write to via the stream-proxy API using bearer token authentication.

extractedEntities.name: Resource Alias Map
extractedEntities.description: Environment variable (RESOURCE_ALIAS_MAP) providing a JSON mapping of resource aliases to RIDs and branches for compute module I/O.

extractedEntities.name: BUILD2_TOKEN
extractedEntities.description: Environment variable containing the bearer token used by compute modules to authenticate API calls to Foundry services.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Proxy API
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's API endpoint (stream-proxy/api/streams/{rid}/branches/{branch}/jsonRecord) for writing JSON records to streaming resources.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology functions that compute modules can implement for custom data processing and generation logic.

keyInsights: Compute modules use Docker containers with the foundry-compute-modules Python package for data generation and processing.
The RESOURCE_ALIAS_MAP environment variable maps human-readable aliases to Foundry resource RIDs and branches.
Pipelines modules receive auto-granted permissions to their input and output resources, simplifying configuration.
Data is written to Foundry streams via the stream-proxy API endpoint with bearer token authentication.
Compute modules support change data capture and configurable throughput settings for streaming outputs.

title: Custom widgets - Parameters and events - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application building tool that serves as the host application for custom widgets, managing state and event binding.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects of a specific Object Type, passable as a typed parameter to custom widgets with an associated OSDK object type.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology type that defines the schema of objects within an ObjectSet parameter.

extractedEntities.name: Widget Parameter
extractedEntities.description: Typed input mechanism for custom widgets supporting boolean, date, number, string, timestamp primitives, arrays, and objectSet types, with a limit of 50 per widget.

extractedEntities.name: Widget Event
extractedEntities.description: Output mechanism enabling custom widgets to communicate back to Workshop, triggering side effects and parameter updates via parameterUpdateIds, limited to 50 per widget.

extractedEntities.name: Bidirectional Widget Communication
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where Workshop passes data to widgets via parameter bindings and widgets update Workshop state by emitting events with parameter update payloads.

extractedEntities.name: Object Set RID
extractedEntities.description: Resource Identifier format (ri.object-set.main.temporary-object-set.*) used to reference object sets in widget parameter values.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology interface type referenced in the context of custom widget parameter compatibility.

extractedEntities.name: CamelCase Parameter IDs
extractedEntities.description: Constraint requiring all widget parameter and event IDs to use camelCase format.

keyInsights: Custom widgets communicate with Workshop through a typed parameter and event system, each limited to 50 items.
Widget parameters support primitive types (boolean, date, number, string, timestamp), arrays, and objectSet types.
Events use parameterUpdateIds to specify which parameters can be modified when the event fires, enabling two-way state sync.
Object set parameters require an associated OSDK object type declaration and use RID-based references.
Struct and object set filter types are currently unsupported as first-class widget parameter types.

title: Ontology SDK - Bootstrap a new TypeScript application with a service user - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Ontology SDK for TypeScript that enables programmatic access to the Ontology from both client-side and server-side applications.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application management interface for creating OSDK applications, generating SDK packages, and managing OAuth credentials.

extractedEntities.name: Service User
extractedEntities.description: An application-level identity automatically created by Developer Console for backend services, enabling Ontology access without end-user authentication.

extractedEntities.name: Confidential OAuth Client
extractedEntities.description: OAuth client type used for server-side OSDK applications that authenticates using a client secret rather than user-interactive flows.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity types selected during OSDK package generation that define which data the application can query.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology write-back operations selected during OSDK generation that the application can invoke.

extractedEntities.name: createConfidentialOauthClient
extractedEntities.description: OSDK function that creates an OAuth client for service user authentication using clientId and clientSecret.

extractedEntities.name: createClient
extractedEntities.description: OSDK function that creates the main client instance by combining a Foundry URL, ontology RID, and authentication provider.

extractedEntities.name: Backend Service Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for headless/server-side applications using service user credentials (client ID + secret) instead of interactive user authentication.

extractedEntities.name: Role
extractedEntities.description: Permission model where the service user must be granted roles to read object type data and execute action types.

keyInsights: Server-side OSDK applications use a confidential OAuth client with client ID and secret for service user authentication.
Developer Console automatically creates a service user when configuring a backend service application type.
OSDK packages are generated per-application with a fixed set of object types and action types that cannot change ontology after generation.
The service user must be explicitly granted permissions to read object types and execute action types via roles.
Client secrets are shown only once during application creation; rotation is possible but breaks existing applications.

DSC (Desired State Configuration) modules enforce and auto-correct AD configuration drift at regular intervals.
AD images use a layered approach with a base Windows image containing security tooling, on which AD-specific configuration is layered.
The immutable image approach reduces entropy, codifies configuration, and aligns with CI/CD workflows.

keyInsights: AIP operationalizes classification rules by translating security guides and FOIA rules into an Ontology, turning rules into tools.
Human+Machine principles are foundational to AIP, ensuring humans remain decision-makers while AI provides scale and speed.
Discrete controls on LLMs mitigate hallucination risks while enabling document context analysis at scale.
AIP enables real-time simulation, inspection, and auditing of classification workflows for accuracy and consistency.
The classification system modernization demonstrates the Ontology pattern of encoding domain rules as machine-actionable structures.

title: Alerting and Detection Strategy Framework

extractedEntities.name: Alerting and Detection Strategy Framework
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's internal framework for designing, implementing, testing, and managing security alerting strategies with documented sections for goals, blind spots, validation, and response plans.

extractedEntities.name: ADS Template Sections

extractedEntities.name: Alert Quality Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: Process of hypothesis generation, peer review, true-positive testing, production deployment, and ongoing issue tracking for detection strategies.

extractedEntities.name: Alert Validation
extractedEntities.description: Requirement that all alerting strategies undergo true-positive testing and durability validation before production deployment.

extractedEntities.name: Version-Controlled ADS
extractedEntities.description: Practice of storing all alert documentation in durable, version-controlled, centralized locations with issue tracking for modifications.

keyInsights: Palantir's ADS framework requires documented sections covering goals, blind spots, validation, false positives, and response plans before production deployment.
All alerts must undergo true-positive testing and peer review as part of a mandatory quality gate.
Alert documentation is stored in version-controlled repositories with issue tracking for modifications, similar to code review workflows.
The framework addresses alert fatigue by requiring rigor in hypothesis generation and testing to prevent low-signal alert flooding.

title: Auditing with osquery: Part One — Introduction to the Linux Audit Framework

extractedEntities.name: Linux Audit Framework
extractedEntities.description: Kernel-level audit subsystem in Linux (since kernel 2.6) that generates audit events for security-relevant operations like file modifications and system calls.

extractedEntities.name: osquery
extractedEntities.description: Open-source endpoint security tool that Palantir uses as an audit consumer for Linux audit events, serving as an alternative to auditd.

extractedEntities.name: Audit Rule Types
extractedEntities.description: Three types of Linux audit rules: control rules (system behavior), file system rules (file/directory watches), and system call rules (syscall logging).

extractedEntities.name: Process Auditing
extractedEntities.description: osquery's capability to record process executions by consuming execve syscall audit events from the Linux kernel.

extractedEntities.name: Socket Auditing
extractedEntities.description: osquery's capability to record network connections by consuming bind and connect syscall audit events.

extractedEntities.name: Audit Consumer
extractedEntities.description: Userland application (such as osquery or auditd) that processes audit events emitted from the Linux kernel's audit subsystem.

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise Endpoint Auditing
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of deploying osquery at enterprise scale with properly tuned audit configurations to balance security visibility with system performance.

keyInsights: osquery and auditd serve nearly identical roles as audit consumers, both processing kernel audit events for logging.
Improperly configured auditing causes performance issues, resource conflicts, and incomplete data that weakens detection.
Linux audit rules cover three categories: control rules for system behavior, file watches for access monitoring, and syscall rules for execution logging.
Palantir deploys osquery at enterprise scale, requiring careful tuning of audit configurations to avoid performance degradation.
